one_sentence_contribution: This paper documents the composition, filtering artifacts, and representational biases of C4, providing early measurement infrastructure for assessing quality in large webtext corpora.
gap_it_leaves: It documents one major corpus in depth but does not track web-text quality longitudinally across time or quantify synthetic-content pollution.
manual_override_reason: The C4 documentation paper is treated as a web-corpus composition and quality-measurement anchor.
key_claim: C4 contains significant unexpected content including patents, machine-generated text, benchmark data contamination, and social biases, while blocklist filtering disproportionately excludes minority voices.
methodology: Corpus analysis documenting metadata provenance, content inspection for machine-generated text and biases, and examination of blocklist filtering effects on the Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus (C4)
limitation: Only some possible issues were examined; personally identifiable information and copyrighted text were not quantified; LangID tools work disproportionately well for English, limiting generalizability to other languages.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not quantify how the documented quality issues in web-scraped corpora compound over time as models trained on such data generate content that re-enters the web.

title: The Rise of AI-Generated Content in Wikipedia
venue: Proceedings of the First Workshop on Advancing Natural Language Processing for Wikipedia
abstract: The rise of AI-generated content in popular information sources raises significant concerns about accountability, accuracy, and bias amplification. Beyond directly impacting consumers, the widespread presence of this content poses questions for the long-term viability of training language models on vast internet sweeps. We use GPTZero, a proprietary AI detector, and Binoculars, an open-source alternative, to establish lower bounds on the presence of AI-generated content in recently created Wikipedia pages. Both detectors reveal a marked increase in AI-generated content in recent pages compared to those from before the release of GPT-3.5. With thresholds calibrated to achieve a 1% false positive rate on pre-GPT-3.5 articles, detectors flag over 5% of newly created English Wikipedia articles as AI-generated, with lower percentages for German, French, and Italian articles. Flagged Wikipedia articles are typically of lower quality and are often self-promotional or partial towards a specific viewpoint on controversial topics.
matched_query: model collapse generative AI recursive training
tldr: GPTZero, a proprietary AI detector, and Binoculars, an open-source alternative, are used to establish lower bounds on the presence of AI-generated content in recently created Wikipedia pages to reveal a marked increase in AI-generated content in recent pages.
manual_core_reason: Wikipedia AI-content rise is a direct online-content prevalence / temporal-change anchor.
one_sentence_contribution: This paper estimates lower bounds on AI-generated content in recently created Wikipedia articles using detectors, offering evidence of synthetic-content prevalence in a major online knowledge platform.
gap_it_leaves: Because it relies on imperfect detectors and a single platform, it cannot fully establish true prevalence trends or contamination levels in broader web training corpora.
manual_override_reason: The Wikipedia paper is a temporal online-content prevalence anchor, best aligned with H in this thesis.
key_claim: AI-generated content in recently created Wikipedia pages has markedly increased since GPT-3.5's release, with detectors finding at least 5% of new pages are AI-generated at a 1% false positive threshold.
methodology: Application of GPTZero (proprietary) and Binoculars (open-source) AI detectors to recently created Wikipedia pages, with thresholds calibrated at 1% false positive rate, comparing pre- and post-GPT-3.5 pages
limitation: Proprietary nature and cost of GPTZero limit scale; compute requirements of Binoculars bound study size; only two detectors were used, limiting generalizability.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not measure how AI-generated Wikipedia content, when re-ingested into web-scraped training corpora, contributes to information degradation and diversity loss in downstream models.

title: WWW - Privacy Policies over Time: Curation and Analysis of a Million-Document Dataset
abstract:
Automated analysis of privacy policies has proved a fruitful research direction, with developments such as automated policy summarization, question answering systems, and compliance detection. So far, prior research has been limited to analysis of privacy policies from a single point in time or from short spans of time, as researchers did not have access to a large-scale, longitudinal, curated dataset. To address this gap, we developed a crawler that discovers, downloads, and extracts archived privacy policies from the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine. Using the crawler and natural language processing, we curated a dataset of 1,071,488 English language privacy policies, spanning over two decades and over 130,000 distinct websites. 
Our analyses of the data show how the privacy policy landscape has changed over time and how websites have reacted to the evolving legal landscape, such as the adoption of privacy seals and the impact of new regulations such as the GDPR. Our results suggest that privacy policies underreport the presence of tracking technologies and third parties. We find that, over the last twenty years, privacy policies have more than doubled in length and the median reading level, while already challenging, has increased modestly.
matched_query: ("privacy policies over time" OR PrivaSeer OR "online activity") AND (web OR internet OR "privacy policy") AND (evolution OR longitudinal OR temporal) AND year_published:[2020 TO 2026]
one_sentence_contribution: This work curates and analyzes a million-document longitudinal dataset of privacy policies, enabling temporal study of web documents at scale.
gap_it_leaves: It measures temporal change in a specific web genre rather than quality shifts in general web training data or AI-generated content contamination.
key_claim: Privacy policies are becoming longer, harder to read, and more ambiguous over time, while severely underreporting usage of third parties and tracking technologies.
methodology: Longitudinal corpus curation of 1,071,488 privacy policies from the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine spanning over two decades, with automated trend surfacing and analysis of tracking technology disclosure
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the evolving nature of boilerplate legal text in web corpora (like privacy policies) contributes to content homogenization and entropy decline in web-scraped training datasets.

title: Online Media Archeology as AI Critique: Wikipedia's Links and Edits as Spatial and Temporal Fields
venue: AoIR Selected Papers of Internet Research
abstract: <jats:p>There have been many impulses to make sense of artificial intelligence, or what AI ‘is.’ We propose that media archeology on Wikipedia can aid in the sensemaking of AI. Wikipedia’s technicity - its linked article ecology together with its edit histories - makes it suitable to perform a media archeology, which already favors spaciousness and complexities. The starting point for the analysis is the ‘Artificial intelligence’ article on Wikipedia. We then operationalize the ‘See also’ section into a link ecology with categorizations and prominence over time, thus allowing us to map and explore the spatial field within which the artificial intelligence article has resided across its temporal dimension. This means approaching Wikipedia as a source of data about not only content but also the connections and ruptures. By ‘ruptures’ we mean changes by deletion or substitution in article associations. Ultimately, taking inspiration from media archeology’s presumption to unearth understandings of media technologies through their multiple dimensions and ruptures, we present our findings in terms of the ‘ideation company’ that AI keeps on Wikipedia. First, we notice the instability of the AI industry. Second, we observe a decline in the prominence of science fiction-related articles over time. The rise of articles under the umbrella of human enhancements can be seen in the most recent periods, as well as the rising prominence of articles related to threat recognition and accountability of AI. In our investigation, Wikipedia emerges as a (substantive) AI sense-making space as well as a media archeology instrument.</jats:p>
matched_query: (Wikipedia OR wiki) AND ("AI-generated content" OR synthetic content) AND (rise OR prevalence OR temporal OR longitudinal) AND year_published:[2023 TO 2026]
one_sentence_contribution: This paper uses Wikipedia link structures and edit histories to develop a media-archeological critique of AI as a sociotechnical concept.
gap_it_leaves: It is interpretive rather than a measurement study of web-text quality, synthetic-content prevalence, provenance systems, or AI-training data curation.

title: Evolution of diversity and dominance of companies in online activity.
abstract: Ever since the web began, the number of websites has been growing exponentially. These websites cover an ever-increasing range of online services that fill a variety of social and economic functions across a growing range of industries. Yet the networked nature of the web, combined with the economics of preferential attachment, increasing returns and global trade, suggest that over the long run a small number of competitive giants are likely to dominate each functional market segment, such as search, retail and social media. Here we perform a large scale longitudinal study to quantify the distribution of attention given in the online environment to competing organisations. In two large online social media datasets, containing more than 10 billion posts and spanning more than a decade, we tally the volume of external links posted towards the organisations' main domain name as a proxy for the online attention they receive. We also use the Common Crawl dataset-which contains the linkage patterns between more than a billion different websites-to study the patterns of link concentration over the past three years across the entire web. Lastly, we showcase the linking between economic, financial and market data by exploring the relationships between online attention on social media and the growth in enterprise value in the electric carmaker Tesla. Our analysis shows that despite the fact that we observe consistent growth in all the macro indicators-the total amount of online attention, in the number of organisations with an online presence, and in the functions they perform-we also observe that a smaller number of organisations account for an ever-increasing proportion of total user attention, usually with one large player dominating each function. These results highlight how evolution of the online economy involves innovation, diversity, and then competitive dominance.
matched_query: ("privacy policies over time" OR PrivaSeer OR "online activity") AND (web OR internet OR "privacy policy") AND (evolution OR longitudinal OR temporal) AND year_published:[2020 TO 2026]
one_sentence_contribution: This paper studies long-run temporal evolution in the diversity and dominance structure of companies' online activity, quantifying changing composition in the web ecosystem over time.
gap_it_leaves: It analyzes market concentration in online activity rather than linguistic quality, provenance, or synthetic contamination of web text used for AI training.
key_claim: Online attention is increasingly concentrated among a small number of dominant domains, with the top 10 domains on Reddit growing from 35% to 60% of all attention between 2006 and 2019.
methodology: Large-scale longitudinal analysis of outbound weblinks from Reddit and Twitter (2006–2019) combined with Common Crawl PageRank data and economic performance data to measure attention concentration across online companies.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how increasing concentration of online attention affects the diversity and representativeness of web-scraped training data for AI models.
theoretical_framework: Preferential attachment and increasing returns economics applied to online attention markets

title: Event Detection in Wikipedia Edit History Improved by Documents Web Based Automatic Assessment
abstract: A majority of current work in events extraction assumes the static nature of relationships in constant expertise knowledge bases. However, in collaborative environments, such as Wikipedia, information and systems are extraordinarily dynamic over time. In this work, we introduce a new approach for extracting complex structures of events from Wikipedia. We advocate a new model to represent events by engaging more than one entities that are generalizable to an arbitrary language. The evolution of an event is captured successfully primarily based on analyzing the user edits records in Wikipedia. Our work presents a basis for a singular class of evolution-aware entity-primarily based enrichment algorithms and will extensively increase the quality of entity accessibility and temporal retrieval for Wikipedia. We formalize this problem case and conduct comprehensive experiments on a real dataset of 1.8 million Wikipedia articles in order to show the effectiveness of our proposed answer. Furthermore, we suggest a new event validation automatic method relying on a supervised model to predict the presence of events in a non-annotated corpus. As the extra document source for event validation, we chose the Web due to its ease of accessibility and wide event coverage. Our outcomes display that we are capable of acquiring 70% precision evaluated on a manually annotated corpus. Ultimately, we conduct a comparison of our strategy versus the Current Event Portal of Wikipedia and discover that our proposed WikipEvent along with the usage of Co-References technique may be utilized to provide new and more data on events.

one_sentence_contribution: This paper improves event detection from Wikipedia edit histories using document-based automatic assessment methods for dynamic knowledge extraction.
gap_it_leaves: It focuses on event extraction from Wikipedia dynamics rather than temporal web-quality measurement, provenance, or AI-generated content prevalence.

title: Consent in Crisis: The Rapid Decline of the AI Data Commons
abstract: General-purpose artificial intelligence (AI) systems are built on massive swathes of public web data, assembled into corpora such as C4, RefinedWeb, and Dolma. To our knowledge, we conduct the first, large-scale, longitudinal audit of the consent protocols for the web domains underlying AI training corpora. Our audit of 14,000 web domains provides an expansive view of crawlable web data and how codified data use preferences are changing over time. We observe a proliferation of AI-specific clauses to limit use, acute differences in restrictions on AI developers, as well as general inconsistencies between websites' expressed intentions in their Terms of Service and their robots.txt. We diagnose these as symptoms of ineffective web protocols, not designed to cope with the widespread re-purposing of the internet for AI. Our longitudinal analyses show that in a single year (2023-2024) there has been a rapid crescendo of data restrictions from web sources, rendering ~5%+ of all tokens in C4, or 28%+ of the most actively maintained, critical sources in C4, fully restricted from use. For Terms of Service crawling restrictions, a full 45% of C4 is now restricted. If respected or enforced, these restrictions are rapidly biasing the diversity, freshness, and scaling laws for general-purpose AI systems. We hope to illustrate the emerging crises in data consent, for both developers and creators. The foreclosure of much of the open web will impact not only commercial AI, but also non-commercial AI and academic research.
matched_query: all:"RefinedWeb" AND all:"web data"
one_sentence_contribution: This paper performs a longitudinal audit of consent and AI-specific usage clauses across 14,000 web domains underlying major training corpora, measuring how the web data commons is changing over time.
gap_it_leaves: It measures shifts in consent and governance signals but does not directly quantify resulting text-quality changes or downstream effects on model training outcomes.
key_claim: Approximately 5% of tokens in C4 and other major AI training corpora have become restricted by robots.txt in less than a year, and nearly 45% carry some form of Terms of Service restrictions.
methodology: Large-scale longitudinal audit of consent protocols (robots.txt and Terms of Service) across 14,000 web domains underlying AI training corpora C4, RefinedWeb, and Dolma.
limitation: The data ecosystem's inconsistencies and omissions between robots.txt and terms of service pages make it difficult to reliably signal or enforce data use preferences.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze how the progressive restriction of high-quality web domains degrades the distributional properties (tail coverage, entropy, diversity) of remaining available training data.

title: Privacy Policies across the Ages: Content of Privacy Policies 1996–2021
venue: ACM Transactions on Privacy and Security
abstract: It is well known that most users do not read privacy policies but almost always tick the box to agree with them. While the length and readability of privacy policies have been well studied and many approaches for policy analysis based on natural language processing have been proposed, existing studies are limited in their depth and scope, often focusing on a small number of data practices at single point in time. In this article, we fill this gap by analyzing the 25-year history of privacy policies using machine learning and natural language processing and presenting a comprehensive analysis of policy contents. Specifically, we collect a large-scale longitudinal corpus of privacy policies from 1996 to 2021 and analyze their content in terms of the data practices they describe, the rights they grant to users, and the rights they reserve for their organizations. We pay particular attention to changes in response to recent privacy regulations such as the GDPR and CCPA. We observe some positive changes, such as reductions in data collection post-GDPR, but also a range of concerning data practices, such as widespread implicit data collection for which users have no meaningful choices or access rights. Our work is an important step toward making privacy policies machine readable on the user side, which would help users match their privacy preferences against the policies offered by web services.

title: "Way back then": A Data-driven View of 25+ years of Web Evolution
venue: Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2022
abstract: Since the inception of the first web page three decades back, the Web has evolved considerably, from static HTML pages in the beginning to the dynamic web pages of today, from mainly the text-based pages of the 1990s to today’s multimedia rich pages, etc.. Although much of this is known anecdotally, to our knowledge, there is no quantitative documentation of the extent and timing of these changes. This paper attempts to address this gap in the literature by looking at the top 100 Alexa websites for over 25 years from the Internet Archive or the “Wayback Machine”, archive.org. We study the changes in popularity, from Geocities and Yahoo! in the mid-to-late 1990s to the likes of Google, Facebook, and Tiktok of today. We also look at different categories of websites and their popularity over the years and find evidence for the decline in popularity of news and education-related websites, which have been replaced by streaming media and social networking sites. We explore the emergence and relative prevalence of different MIME-types (text vs. image vs. video vs. javascript and json) and study whether the use of text on the Internet is declining.

gap_it_leaves: It characterizes temporal change in the web broadly but does not connect those shifts to AI-data contamination, text quality, or training curation decisions.
key_claim: Over 25+ years, web content has shifted from news and education-related websites toward streaming media and social networking sites, with major changes in dominant platforms.
methodology: Longitudinal quantitative analysis of the top 100 Alexa websites over 25+ years using archived snapshots from the Internet Archive (Wayback Machine).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze how the decline of informational web content and rise of multimedia/social networking platforms affects the diversity and information quality of web-scraped AI training corpora.

title: Collective Response to Media Coverage of the COVID-19 Pandemic on Reddit and Wikipedia: Mixed-Methods Analysis
abstract: Background The exposure and consumption of information during epidemic outbreaks may alter people’s risk perception and trigger behavioral changes, which can ultimately affect the evolution of the disease. It is thus of utmost importance to map the dissemination of information by mainstream media outlets and the public response to this information. However, our understanding of this exposure-response dynamic during the COVID-19 pandemic is still limited. Objective The goal of this study is to characterize the media coverage and collective internet response to the COVID-19 pandemic in four countries: Italy, the United Kingdom, the United States, and Canada. Methods We collected a heterogeneous data set including 227,768 web-based news articles and 13,448 YouTube videos published by mainstream media outlets, 107,898 user posts and 3,829,309 comments on the social media platform Reddit, and 278,456,892 views of COVID-19–related Wikipedia pages. To analyze the relationship between media coverage, epidemic progression, and users’ collective web-based response, we considered a linear regression model that predicts the public response for each country given the amount of news exposure. We also applied topic modelling to the data set using nonnegative matrix factorization. Results Our results show that public attention, quantified as user activity on Reddit and active searches on Wikipedia pages, is mainly driven by media coverage; meanwhile, this activity declines rapidly while news exposure and COVID-19 incidence remain high. Furthermore, using an unsupervised, dynamic topic modeling approach, we show that while the levels of attention dedicated to different topics by media outlets and internet users are in good accordance, interesting deviations emerge in their temporal patterns. Conclusions Overall, our findings offer an additional key to interpret public perception and response to the current global health emergency and raise questions about the effects of attention saturation on people’s collective awareness and risk perception and thus on their tendencies toward behavioral change.

title: Neural Theory-of-Mind? On the Limits of Social Intelligence in Large LMs
abstract: Social intelligence and Theory of Mind (TOM), i.e., the ability to reason about the different mental states, intents, and reactions of all people involved, allows humans to effectively navigate and understand everyday social interactions. As NLP systems are used in increasingly complex social situations, their ability to grasp social dynamics becomes crucial.In this work, we examine the open question of social intelligence and Theory of Mind in modern NLP systems from an empirical and theorybased perspective. We show that one of today's largest language models (GPT-3; Brown et al., 2020) lacks this kind of social intelligence out-of-the box, using two tasks: SocialIQa (Sap et al., 2019), which measure models' ability to understand intents and reactions of participants of social interactions, and ToMi (Le, Boureau, and Nickel, 2019), which measures whether models can infer mental states and realities of participants of situations.Our results show that models struggle substantially at these Theory of Mind tasks, with well-below-human accuracies of 55% and 60% on SocialIQa and ToMi, respectively. To conclude, we draw on theories from pragmatics to contextualize this shortcoming of large language models, by examining the limitations stemming from their data, neural architecture, and training paradigms. Challenging the prevalent narrative that only scale is needed, we posit that person-centric NLP approaches might be more effective towards neural Theory of Mind.

one_sentence_contribution: Examines the limits of large language models' social intelligence and theory-of-mind capabilities, highlighting substantial shortcomings in socially grounded reasoning.
gap_it_leaves: It diagnoses weaknesses in LLM social reasoning but does not offer a broad multimodal or behaviorally grounded remedy for those limitations.
key_claim: Out-of-the-box GPT-3 models lag behind humans by over 30% on social intelligence benchmarks and struggle with mental state reasoning compared to factual questions.
methodology: Evaluated GPT-3 variants (Ada, Curie, Davinci) on SocialIQA and ToMI benchmarks via API; compared zero-shot and few-shot performance against human baselines and fine-tuned models.
limitation: Authors acknowledge they only evaluated GPT-3 via API and lacked access to larger models like PaLM or computational resources for open-source alternatives like OPT.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not empirically test whether augmenting training data with physically-verified authentic social behavioral data would improve Theory of Mind capabilities.
theoretical_framework: Pragmatics-based perspective on LLM pretraining limitations for Theory of Mind

title: CoMMET: To What Extent Can LLMs Perform Theory of Mind Tasks?
abstract: Theory of Mind (ToM)-the ability to reason about the mental states of oneself and others-is a cornerstone of human social intelligence. As Large Language Models (LLMs) become ubiquitous in real-world applications, validating their capacity for this level of social reasoning is essential for effective and natural interactions. However, existing benchmarks for assessing ToM in LLMs are limited; most rely solely on text inputs and focus narrowly on belief-related tasks. In this paper, we propose a new multimodal benchmark dataset, CoMMET, a Comprehensive Mental states and Moral Evaluation Task inspired by the Theory of Mind Booklet Task. CoMMET expands the scope of evaluation by covering a broader range of mental states and introducing multi-turn testing. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first multimodal dataset to evaluate ToM in a multi-turn conversational setting. Through a comprehensive assessment of LLMs across different families and sizes, we analyze the strengths and limitations of current models and identify directions for future improvement. Our work offers a deeper understanding of the social cognitive capabilities of modern LLMs.
matched_query: all:"social reasoning" AND all:"benchmark" AND all:"language model"
one_sentence_contribution: Proposes CoMMET as an expanded benchmark for evaluating LLM theory-of-mind abilities beyond narrow text-only belief tasks.
gap_it_leaves: It broadens benchmark coverage but still centers on evaluation rather than collecting real-world human social interaction data for training.
key_claim: LLMs show uneven Theory of Mind capabilities, performing well on emotion and desire tasks but struggling with complex multi-state reasoning and moral reasoning in multimodal settings.
methodology: Proposed CoMMET, a multimodal benchmark inspired by the Theory of Mind Booklet Task covering all ATOMS mental states plus moral reasoning; evaluated models including LLaMA-3.2-11B and LLaMA-4-Maverick across multiple conversational turns with feedback.
limitation: Authors note issues with image generation quality including incorrect spatial relationships, and one story turn was incorrectly categorized.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the observed ToM deficits in LLMs are attributable to the lack of authentic human social interaction data in training corpora versus architectural limitations.

title: Social-R1: Towards Human-like Social Reasoning in LLMs
abstract: While large language models demonstrate remarkable capabilities across numerous domains, social intelligence - the capacity to perceive social cues, infer mental states, and generate appropriate responses - remains a critical challenge, particularly for enabling effective human-AI collaboration and developing AI that truly serves human needs. Current models often rely on superficial patterns rather than genuine social reasoning. We argue that cultivating human-like social intelligence requires training with challenging cases that resist shortcut solutions. To this end, we introduce ToMBench-Hard, an adversarial benchmark designed to provide hard training examples for social reasoning. Building on this, we propose Social-R1, a reinforcement learning framework that aligns model reasoning with human cognition through multi-dimensional rewards. Unlike outcome-based RL, Social-R1 supervises the entire reasoning process, enforcing structural alignment, logical integrity, and information density. Results show that our approach enables a 4B parameter model to surpass much larger counterparts and generalize robustly across eight diverse benchmarks. These findings demonstrate that challenging training cases with trajectory-level alignment offer a path toward efficient and reliable social intelligence.
matched_query: all:"social reasoning" AND all:"benchmark" AND all:"language model"
one_sentence_contribution: Argues for and develops methods toward more human-like social reasoning in LLMs, emphasizing richer social cues and human-centered signals rather than superficial pattern matching.
gap_it_leaves: It motivates human-like social reasoning but does not by itself resolve how to gather large-scale, ecologically valid social data for training and evaluation.
key_claim: Training with adversarially hard social reasoning examples via reinforcement learning from verifiable feedback significantly improves LLMs' genuine social reasoning and robustness over models relying on superficial pattern matching.
methodology: Introduced ToMBench-Hard adversarial benchmark; trained Social-R1 model using reinforcement learning from verifiable feedback (RLVF) on hard social reasoning examples; conducted stage-wise diagnosis using Social Information Processing (SIP) framework and perturbation analysis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether incorporating authentic human social behavioral data rather than adversarially curated text-based examples could provide more robust and transferable social reasoning capabilities.

title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Models with Self-Generated Instructions
abstract: Yizhong Wang, Yeganeh Kordi, Swaroop Mishra, Alisa Liu, Noah A. Smith, Daniel Khashabi, Hannaneh Hajishirzi. Proceedings of the 61st Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers). 2023.

title: OpenAssistant Conversations -- Democratizing Large Language Model Alignment
abstract: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences has proven to drastically improve usability and has driven rapid adoption as demonstrated by ChatGPT. Alignment techniques such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) greatly reduce the required skill and domain knowledge to effectively harness the capabilities of LLMs, increasing their accessibility and utility across various domains. However, state-of-the-art alignment techniques like RLHF rely on high-quality human feedback data, which is expensive to create and often remains proprietary. In an effort to democratize research on large-scale alignment, we release OpenAssistant Conversations, a human-generated, human-annotated assistant-style conversation corpus consisting of 161,443 messages in 35 different languages, annotated with 461,292 quality ratings, resulting in over 10,000 complete and fully annotated conversation trees. The corpus is a product of a worldwide crowd-sourcing effort involving over 13,500 volunteers. Models trained on OpenAssistant Conversations show consistent improvements on standard benchmarks over respective base models. We release our code and data under a fully permissive licence.
matched_query: data quality LLM training curation pretraining scaling
tldr: OpenAssistant Conversations is released, a human-generated, human-annotated assistant-style conversation corpus consisting of 161,443 messages in 35 different languages, annotated with 461,292 quality ratings, resulting in over 10,000 complete and fully annotated conversation trees.
manual_core_reason: OpenAssistant Conversations is a human / open alignment-data anchor for fine-tuning composition.
one_sentence_contribution: Presents OpenAssistant as an open, community-driven platform and dataset for collecting human alignment conversations to support supervised fine-tuning and RLHF.
gap_it_leaves: It opens human preference data collection and alignment pipelines but does not cleanly separate how data quality, provenance, and composition each affect downstream model behavior.
manual_override_reason: OpenAssistant Conversations is a foundational open alignment-data corpus and belongs in J for this thesis.
key_claim: A large-scale open-source dataset of human-generated conversations for LLM alignment can be crowdsourced to democratize RLHF research.
methodology: Crowdsourced collection of human-generated assistant conversations with quality guidelines, followed by supervised fine-tuning and RLHF training on the collected data.
limitation: Reward models were trained on rankings of human-generated messages rather than model-generated messages as in InstructGPT, which may explain why RLHF models did not show the same uniform improvement over SFT models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether crowdsourced conversational data exhibits the same diversity and authenticity properties as naturally occurring human social behavioral data versus web-scraped text.

title: LIMA: Less Is More for Alignment
abstract: Large language models are trained in two stages: (1) unsupervised pretraining from raw text, to learn general-purpose representations, and (2) large scale instruction tuning and reinforcement learning, to better align to end tasks and user preferences. We measure the relative importance of these two stages by training LIMA, a 65B parameter LLaMa language model fine-tuned with the standard supervised loss on only 1,000 carefully curated prompts and responses, without any reinforcement learning or human preference modeling. LIMA demonstrates remarkably strong performance, learning to follow specific response formats from only a handful of examples in the training data, including complex queries that range from planning trip itineraries to speculating about alternate history. Moreover, the model tends to generalize well to unseen tasks that did not appear in the training data. In a controlled human study, responses from LIMA are either equivalent or strictly preferred to GPT-4 in 43% of cases; this statistic is as high as 58% when compared to Bard and 65% versus DaVinci003, which was trained with human feedback. Taken together, these results strongly suggest that almost all knowledge in large language models is learned during pretraining, and only limited instruction tuning data is necessary to teach models to produce high quality output.
matched_query: LIMA quality over quantity instruction tuning alignment
manual_core_reason: LIMA is a quality-over-quantity fine-tuning anchor for Beat 4 data composition.
one_sentence_contribution: Demonstrates that a small, carefully curated instruction-tuning set can strongly align a large language model, emphasizing quality over quantity in fine-tune data composition.
gap_it_leaves: It shows the power of curated small-scale alignment data but does not provide systematic composition ablations across human, synthetic, and socially grounded datasets.
key_claim: Fine-tuning a 65B LLaMA model on only 1,000 carefully curated examples without RLHF produces competitive performance with state-of-the-art models, supporting the Superficial Alignment Hypothesis.
methodology: Standard supervised fine-tuning of LLaMA-65B on 1,000 manually curated prompt-response pairs, evaluated via human preference comparisons against GPT-4, DaVinci003, and Alpaca.
limitation: The mental effort to construct high-quality examples is significant and difficult to scale, and LIMA is not as robust as product-grade models—adversarial prompts or unlucky decoding can produce weak responses.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the quality of pretraining data (e.g., web-scraped vs. physically-verified human behavioral data) modulates the effectiveness of minimal alignment fine-tuning.

title: WizardLM: Empowering large pre-trained language models to follow complex instructions
abstract: Training large language models (LLMs) with open-domain instruction following data brings colossal success. However, manually creating such instruction data is very time-consuming and labor-intensive. Moreover, humans may struggle to produce high-complexity instructions. In this paper, we show an avenue for creating large amounts of instruction data with varying levels of complexity using LLM instead of humans. Starting with an initial set of instructions, we use our proposed Evol-Instruct to rewrite them step by step into more complex instructions. Then, we mix all generated instruction data to fine-tune LLaMA. We call the resulting model WizardLM. Human evaluations on a complexity-balanced test bed and Vicuna's testset show that instructions from Evol-Instruct are superior to human-created ones. By analyzing the human evaluation results of the high complexity part, we demonstrate that outputs from our WizardLM are preferred to outputs from OpenAI ChatGPT. In GPT-4 automatic evaluation, WizardLM achieves more than 90\% capacity of ChatGPT on 17 out of 29 skills. Even though WizardLM still lags behind ChatGPT in some aspects, our findings suggest that fine-tuning with AI-evolved instructions is a promising direction for enhancing LLMs. Our code and data are public at https://github.com/nlpxucan/WizardLM
matched_query: social commonsense reasoning language model evaluation
tldr: The findings suggest that fine-tuning with AI-evolved instructions is a promising direction for enhancing LLMs, and it is demonstrated that outputs from the authors' WizardLM are preferred to outputs from OpenAI ChatGPT.
manual_core_reason: WizardLM is a high-signal instruction-data construction / complexity-mixture anchor.
one_sentence_contribution: Introduces Evol-Instruct, a method for generating synthetic instructions of increasing complexity to improve instruction-following through fine-tune data design.
gap_it_leaves: It advances synthetic data generation for instruction tuning but does not compare synthetic versus human social data in a targeted social-reasoning ablation setting.
manual_override_reason: WizardLM is a direct instruction-data generation / complexity-mixture anchor.
key_claim: Evol-Instruct, which uses LLMs to iteratively rewrite instructions into more complex versions, produces training data that enables WizardLM to outperform Alpaca and Vicuna on multiple benchmarks.
methodology: Proposed Evol-Instruct pipeline that evolves seed instructions through in-depth and in-breadth rewriting using an LLM, filters low-quality outputs, then fine-tunes LLaMA on the evolved instruction data.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether LLM-generated evolutionary instruction data converges toward homogeneous patterns over iterations, potentially reducing diversity compared to authentic human-generated instructions.

title: From Quantity to Quality: Boosting LLM Performance with Self-Guided Data Selection for Instruction Tuning
venue: North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics
abstract: Ming Li, Yong Zhang, Zhitao Li, Jiuhai Chen, Lichang Chen, Ning Cheng, Jianzong Wang, Tianyi Zhou, Jing Xiao. Proceedings of the 2024 Conference of the North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics: Human Language Technologies (Volume 1: Long Papers). 2024.
matched_query: LIMA quality over quantity instruction tuning alignment
tldr: This work introduces a self-guided methodology for LLMs to autonomously discern and select cherry samples from open-source datasets, effectively minimizing manual curation and potential cost for instruction tuning an LLM.
manual_core_reason: From Quantity to Quality is a direct data-selection / instruction-tuning composition paper.
one_sentence_contribution: Introduces a self-guided data selection method for instruction tuning that emphasizes higher-quality subsets over larger raw quantities to improve LLM performance.
gap_it_leaves: It does not directly establish how its selection strategy generalizes across domains, model sizes, or real-vs-synthetic social data settings.
manual_override_reason: From Quantity to Quality is a direct instruction-tuning data-selection / quality-vs-quantity anchor.
key_claim: With only 10% of selectively chosen instruction tuning data guided by the Instruction-Following Difficulty metric, models exceed performance of models trained on the full dataset.
methodology: Proposed Instruction-Following Difficulty (IFD) metric to score training samples, trained pre-experienced models on LLaMA-2, then selected cherry samples from Alpaca and WizardLM datasets for fine-tuning
limitation: The pre-experienced model training phase is inconvenient and makes the method hard to directly deploy in real-world scenarios.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether IFD-based selection can detect or mitigate degraded or low-diversity patterns in web-scraped training corpora.

title: Hybrid Training Approaches for LLMs: Leveraging Real and Synthetic Data to Enhance Model Performance in Domain-Specific Applications
abstract: Hybrid model (real + synthetic) consistently outperforms both real-only and synthetic-only in therapy/counseling domain. Demonstrates superior contextual sensitivity and emotional understanding. Direct experimental precedent for L_auth pilot study: three-arm comparison (base, real-only, hybrid) in a social/emotional domain. CampusGo experiment should follow this design template. Search hints: Hybrid Training real synthetic data domain-specific LLM; arxiv 2410.09168; Zhezherau Yanockin hybrid fine-tuning therapy counseling
matched_query: Hybrid Training real synthetic data domain-specific LLM
manual_core_reason: Direct experimental precedent for L_auth pilot study: three-arm comparison (base, real-only, hybrid) in a social/emotional domain. CampusGo experiment should follow this design template.
one_sentence_contribution: Compares real-only, synthetic-only, and hybrid fine-tuning data in a therapy/counseling setting and finds that combining real and synthetic data yields better domain performance than either source alone.
gap_it_leaves: It does not clarify which properties of the human data drive gains or whether improvements transfer beyond domain-specific response quality to broader social reasoning.
key_claim: A hybrid fine-tuning approach combining real-world transcribed interactions with synthetic data consistently outperforms models fine-tuned with real data alone in therapeutic dialogue generation.
methodology: Fine-tuned three LLM variants (base, real-data-only, hybrid) on a dataset combining transcribed real therapeutic interactions with synthetic persona-driven sessions; evaluated via GPT-4o-simulated conversations scored for empathy and relevance across 50 therapeutic scenarios
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze whether synthetic data introduces distributional biases or entropy loss compared to authentic human behavioral data in therapeutic settings.

title: Synth-Empathy: Towards High-Quality Synthetic Empathy Data
abstract: Proposes synthetic empathy data pipeline with quality and diversity selection modules. Achieves SOTA empathetic response performance. Relevant for understanding what synthetic social data can vs cannot do. Bounded synthetic success in social domain: shows synthetic empathy data can work for response quality but does not test whether it transfers to genuine ToM or behavioral prediction. Search hints: Synth-Empathy synthetic empathy data quality diversity; arxiv 2407.21669
matched_query: Synth-Empathy synthetic empathy data quality diversity
manual_core_reason: Bounded synthetic success in social domain: shows synthetic empathy data can work for response quality but does not test whether it transfers to genuine ToM or behavioral prediction.
one_sentence_contribution: Presents a pipeline for generating and selecting high-quality, diverse synthetic empathy data that improves empathetic response modeling.
gap_it_leaves: It leaves open whether synthetic empathy data supports deeper social reasoning or only surface-level empathetic response generation.
key_claim: LLM-generated synthetic empathetic data, filtered through quality and diversity selection, enables models to outperform all previous models including those trained on human-labeled empathetic data.
methodology: Developed Synth-Empathy pipeline using LLM-based data generation with quality scoring and diversity selection modules, then fine-tuned models on curated synthetic empathetic data
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether synthetic empathy data captures the full range of authentic human emotional expression or whether it narrows the distribution of empathetic responses compared to real human-labeled data.

title: How Far Can In-Context Alignment Go? Exploring the State of In-Context Alignment
abstract: Recent studies have demonstrated that In-Context Learning (ICL), through the use of specific demonstrations, can align Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences known as In-Context Alignment (ICA), indicating that models can comprehend human instructions without requiring parameter adjustments. However, the exploration of the mechanism and applicability of ICA remains limited. In this paper, we begin by dividing the context text used in ICA into three categories: format, system prompt, and example. Through ablation experiments, we investigate the effectiveness of each part in enabling ICA to function effectively. We then examine how variants in these parts impact the model's alignment performance. Our findings indicate that the example part is crucial for enhancing the model's alignment capabilities, with changes in examples significantly affecting alignment performance. We also conduct a comprehensive evaluation of ICA's zero-shot capabilities in various alignment tasks. The results indicate that compared to parameter fine-tuning methods, ICA demonstrates superior performance in knowledge-based tasks and tool-use tasks. However, it still exhibits certain limitations in areas such as multi-turn dialogues and instruction following.
matched_query: LoRA QLoRA fine-tune data composition ablation alignment
one_sentence_contribution: Studies in-context alignment by decomposing prompt context into components such as format and system prompt rather than varying fine-tuning data composition.
gap_it_leaves: It does not address training-data mixture, curation, or synthetic-versus-human fine-tuning ablations central to the thesis taxonomy.

title: Fine-tuning with HED-IT: The impact of human post-editing for dialogical language models
abstract: Automatic methods for generating and gathering linguistic data have proven effective for fine-tuning Language Models (LMs) in languages less resourced than English.Still, while there has been emphasis on data quantity, less attention has been given to its quality.In this work, we investigate the impact of human intervention on machine-generated data when fine-tuning dialogical models.In particular, we study (1) whether post-edited dialogues exhibit higher perceived quality compared to the originals that were automatically generated; (2) whether fine-tuning with post-edited dialogues results in noticeable differences in the generated outputs; and (3) whether post-edited dialogues influence the outcomes when considering the parameter size of the LMs.To this end we created HED-IT, a large-scale dataset where machine-generated dialogues are paired with the version post-edited by humans.Using both the edited and unedited portions of HED-IT, we fine-tuned three different sizes of an LM.Results from both human and automatic evaluation show that the different quality of training data is clearly perceived and it has an impact also on the models trained on such data.Additionally, our findings indicate that larger models are less sensitive to data quality, whereas this has a crucial impact on smaller models.These results enhance our comprehension of the impact of human intervention on training data in the development of high-quality LMs.
matched_query: "From Quantity to Quality" instruction tuning data selection
one_sentence_contribution: Evaluates how human post-editing of machine-generated dialogues affects downstream dialog model fine-tuning quality, directly testing data composition quality versus raw synthetic generation.
gap_it_leaves: It does not quantify the cost-benefit tradeoff of post-editing at scale or isolate which edits matter most for broader instruction-tuning performance.
key_claim: Human post-editing of machine-generated dialogues yields higher perceived quality data and improves fine-tuned dialogical model outputs, though effects vary with model size.
methodology: Generated Italian dialogues via ChatGPT, had human annotators post-edit them to create HED-IT corpus (original vs. post-edited), then fine-tuned Pythia models in three sizes and evaluated via automatic and human evaluation
limitation: Resource is only available in Italian; testing was limited to Pythia models; prompting autoregressive models can lead to hallucinations; data was generated by a single LLM (ChatGPT).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether human post-editing restores authentic social behavioral signals lost in machine-generated text or whether it merely corrects surface-level artifacts.

title: Fine-tuning with HED-IT: The impact of human post-editing for dialogical language models
abstract: Automatic methods for generating and gathering linguistic data have proven effective for fine-tuning Language Models (LMs) in languages less resourced than English. Still, while there has been emphasis on data quantity, less attention has been given to its quality. In this work, we investigate the impact of human intervention on machine-generated data when fine-tuning dialogical models. In particular, we study (1) whether post-edited dialogues exhibit higher perceived quality compared to the originals that were automatically generated; (2) whether fine-tuning with post-edited dialogues results in noticeable differences in the generated outputs; and (3) whether post-edited dialogues influence the outcomes when considering the parameter size of the LMs. To this end we created HED-IT, a large-scale dataset where machine-generated dialogues are paired with the version post-edited by humans. Using both the edited and unedited portions of HED-IT, we fine-tuned three different sizes of an LM. Results from both human and automatic evaluation show that the different quality of training data is clearly perceived and it has an impact also on the models trained on such data. Additionally, our findings indicate that larger models are less sensitive to data quality, whereas this has a crucial impact on smaller models. These results enhance our comprehension of the impact of human intervention on training data in the development of high-quality LMs.
matched_query: "From Quantity to Quality" instruction tuning data selection
one_sentence_contribution: Evaluates how human post-editing of machine-generated dialogues affects downstream dialog model fine-tuning quality, directly testing data composition quality versus raw synthetic generation.
gap_it_leaves: It does not quantify the cost-benefit tradeoff of post-editing at scale or isolate which edits matter most for broader instruction-tuning performance.
key_claim: Human post-editing of machine-generated dialogues improves both perceived data quality and the quality of fine-tuned dialogical language models.
methodology: Created HED-IT, a corpus of machine-generated Italian dialogues paired with human post-edited counterparts, then fine-tuned Pythia models in three sizes and evaluated via automatic and human evaluation.
limitation: Resource is only in Italian; data generated by prompting ChatGPT may contain hallucinations; testing was limited to only a subset of possible models (Pythia).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether human post-editing can counteract systematic diversity loss or entropy decline in web-scraped training corpora at scale.

title: Enhancing Chat Language Models by Scaling High-quality Instructional Conversations
abstract: Fine-tuning on instruction data has been widely validated as an effective practice for implementing chat language models like ChatGPT. Scaling the diversity and quality of such data, although straightforward, stands a great chance of leading to improved performance. This paper aims to improve the upper bound of open-source models further. We first provide a systematically designed, diverse, informative, large-scale dataset of instructional conversations, UltraChat, which does not involve human queries. Our objective is to capture the breadth of interactions that a human might have with an AI assistant and employs a comprehensive framework to generate multi-turn conversation iteratively. UltraChat contains 1.5 million high-quality multi-turn dialogues and covers a wide range of topics and instructions. Our statistical analysis of UltraChat reveals its superiority in various key metrics, including scale, average length, diversity, coherence, etc., solidifying its position as a leading open-source dataset. Building upon UltraChat, we fine-tune a LLaMA model to create a powerful conversational model, UltraLLaMA. Our evaluations indicate that UltraLLaMA consistently outperforms other open-source models, including Vicuna, the previously recognized state-of-the-art open-source model. The dataset and the model will be publicly released\footnote{\url{https://github.com/thunlp/UltraChat}}.
matched_query: LIMA quality over quantity instruction tuning alignment
one_sentence_contribution: Argues that scaling diverse, high-quality instructional conversations improves chat model performance, emphasizing data composition and quality in instruction tuning.
gap_it_leaves: It does not cleanly disentangle the effects of data quality, diversity, and quantity or compare human-authored against synthetic alternatives in controlled ablations.
key_claim: Scaling diverse, high-quality synthetic instructional conversations (UltraChat) for fine-tuning LLaMA produces a chat model (UltraLLaMA) that outperforms existing open-source models across multiple benchmarks.
methodology: Systematically generated a large-scale multi-turn instructional conversation dataset (UltraChat) using ChatGPT without human queries, drawing on C4 corpus materials, then fine-tuned LLaMA to create UltraLLaMA and evaluated with automatic benchmarks and ChatGPT-based evaluation.
limitation: Evaluation of LLM response quality may have biases; ChatGPT rather than GPT-4 was used for automatic evaluation; UltraChat supports only English.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether fully synthetic instructional data introduces information degradation or diversity loss compared to authentic human conversational data.

title: What Are the Internal and External Applications of Conversational Data in Large Language Models?
abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) have become increasingly integrated into the internet, the collection and use of conversational data from LLM interactions have introduced both new potential and concerns.The paper reviews both internal and external applications of LLM conversational data in light of current regulations.The paper also highlights the conversational dataset's preprocessing step of filtering and labeling, which are shown to enhance the efficiency in both internal and external applications.Internally, this paper describes the use of conversational data for fine-tuning and model improvement.This paper will cover the main types of fine-tuning, including supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback, and multi-turn dialogue supervised fine-tuning.Externally, conversational data supports user analytics and behavior predictions across both large and small populations.But both applications are heavily dependent on various elements of conversational data around quality and quantity.In addition, constraints on accessing high-quality data continue to hinder the development of conversation-based user analytics.The paper addresses different constraining factors that create a disparity between large and small AI companies, including current LLMs' privacy policies and governance frameworks, such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), and the EU Digital Markets Act (DMA).Ultimately, the areas of improvement that emerge in this paper lead to a set of recommendations for SMEs and policymakers to leverage between LLMs' beneficial capabilities and privacy concerns.
matched_query: Self-Instruct WizardLM OpenAssistant instruction tuning data selection
one_sentence_contribution: Reviews internal and external uses of conversational data from LLM interactions, highlighting preprocessing, filtering, labeling, and regulatory concerns around managing conversational-data pipelines.
gap_it_leaves: It remains a high-level review and does not provide controlled evidence on how different conversational-data sources or provenance constraints affect model behavior.
key_claim: Conversational data from LLM interactions serves dual roles: internally for fine-tuning and model improvement, and externally for user analytics and behavior prediction.
methodology: Literature review of internal and external applications of LLM conversational data, including supervised fine-tuning, RLHF, and multi-turn dialogue supervised fine-tuning, contextualized within current regulations.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether LLM-generated conversational data suffers from entropy decline or tail collapse compared to physically-verified authentic human behavioral data.
